next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Tommy was alone on the deck for the first time since Saturday morning. Monday afternoon, sun low enough to turn the lake copper, and maybe fifteen minutes before somebody came out to check on him or ask if he wanted another round of Mallick's questions or start talking about Martin in that careful tone everybody had found since the body came out of the bedroom.

He had dragged one of Martin's Adirondack chairs to the rail so he could put his feet up and look at the water. A warm can of Busch Light sat in both hands. He wasn't drinking it. He was palming it the way he palmed a football before a snap drill, not because the beer mattered but because empty hands felt worse.

It had been two nights since Martin. Not even three full days, and the house already felt like a locker room after a loss nobody knew how to explain. Men kept picking up cups, setting them down, starting sentences, dropping them halfway through. Paul had spent the morning in Martin's study doing whatever Paul did when he was processing, which usually meant turning grief into categories. Joel was somewhere in the house, still angry at a man who couldn't catch any more of it. Doug had been on the couch with the same magazine page since breakfast. Nate was in the basement with his phone, which Tommy was trying not to think about.

Mallick had gone back to Detroit Lakes. She was working the case, supposedly. Tommy didn't buy it. That woman filed people the way Tommy filed opponent tendencies. She filed tells and mistakes and weak side and strong side. She'd be back.

The screen door opened and ban...
open_hooks_to_carry: Paul in Martin's room going through his things — the intimacy and invasiveness of handling a dead friend's belongings
Paul finds Martin's will in the study: Martin left Tommy the lake house, not Paul
Memories of the Martin-Tommy dynamic across twenty-five years: protectiveness, tolerance, the way Martin always ensured Tommy came back
Paul begins to suspect a deeper bond — something that created obligation, not just friendship
The comedy in Paul's indignation should be real: he's also hurt that his best friend left the house to someone else
prev_tail_excerpt:
...for Nate to lean in for the phone and get back out before Paul looked up. She could make those fit.

The Viagra removal still snagged. Somebody had to go into Martin's room, find the pills, get back out, get downstairs to the half bath, and flush them before the house fully woke. That was more movement. It also meant more chance to run into Paul in the hall or hear him breathing hard over the bed or have him look up at exactly the wrong second. Tommy was not a small man. She pictured him trying to make himself narrow enough for the house not to notice and almost laughed again. It was possible. It was not easy.

She stood at the top of the stairs and listened. Old houses lied about sound. A tick in the pipe could be a footstep. A settling pop from downstairs could sound like a door on the hall. Paul could have heard movement and been wrong about where it was coming from. That helped. It did not solve the room.

Back in the kitchen, she wrote under bedroom traffic: "Can miss one in the dark. Hard to miss the rest." Then she circled the sentence because it wasn't a conclusion. It was the problem.

She closed the notebook, rinsed her coffee mug, dried it, and set it on the counter next to the coffeemaker because she'd want it first thing and it was already clean and why open the cabinet twice. She pressed the foil down over the casserole dish with both hands, just to have a task for them, then turned off the kitchen light and went upstairs to the guest bedroom the deputies had cleared for her.

The third step from the top creaked under her weight, thin and accusing in the dark.
secondary_character_beats: Paul's memories of Martin should include at least one that is purely funny and purely kind — the reader needs to grieve Martin too

setups_to_plant.description: Martin left Tommy the lake house in his will. This fact, combined with the fire document, will be the emotional trigger in Paul's confrontation with Tommy in Ch 17.

state_deltas_required: Paul's discovery that Martin's central loyalty was not to him but to Tommy
Paul's grief shifts from loss to suspicion — Martin was hiding something about Tommy, and the will is proof it wasn't casual
